topic: Data Preprocessing: Handling Outliers, Missing Values, and Skewed Data

questions.scenario: Real-world Industrial Problem Statement: A predictive maintenance system for a wind farm processes high-frequency telemetry data (vibration, temperature, wind speed) to predict bearing failures. The vibration amplitude features are highly right-skewed and contain negative values due to zero-point calibration drifts in older sensors. Machine learning models like Support Vector Machines (SVM) and linear regressors are yielding poor performance because they assume normally distributed, homoscedastic feature spaces. The pipeline requires a preprocessing step that stabilizes variance, mitigates extreme skewness, handles negative values natively, and maintains numerical stability without arbitrary data shifts.
questions.question: Which transformation strategy is mathematically most appropriate for this scenario?
options: A) Apply a Box-Cox transformation after adding a manually tuned constant offset C to make all values strictly positive.
B) Use a Yeo-Johnson transformation followed by a Z-score Standardization (StandardScaler).
C) Perform a standard Logarithmic transformation log(x + 1) followed by MinMax Scaling.
D) Use a RobustScaler to scale the features based on the Interquartile Range (IQR) without applying any distribution shape transformations.
questions.correct_answer_text: B) Use a Yeo-Johnson transformation followed by a Z-score Standardization (StandardScaler).
questions.explanation: The Yeo-Johnson transformation is a generalization of the Box-Cox power transformation that natively handles zero and negative values, making it ideal for the sensor drift scenario without needing arbitrary manual offset shifts. While Box-Cox requires strictly positive data (x > 0), adding an arbitrary constant offset C can heavily distort the distribution tail and alter physical relationships. Log(x+1) only handles non-negative values (x >= 0) and fails if calibration drift drops values below -1. Following Yeo-Johnson with Z-score standardization ensures the transformed features are normally distributed and centered with unit variance, which is a mathematical requirement for distance-based models like SVM.

questions.scenario: Real-world Industrial Problem Statement: An automated credit card fraud detection system processes streaming transactions in real-time. The dataset features 120 continuous variables derived from PCA, along with several categorical variables. Outliers in this system represent potential fraudulent activities, which are highly multivariate (interactions between multiple features rather than extreme values in a single feature). The system operates under a strict latency budget where inference must take less than 30 milliseconds per transaction, and the model must handle high-dimensional, sparse data without suffering from the curse of dimensionality.
questions.question: Which outlier detection algorithm represents the best trade-off between execution speed, high-dimensional performance, and low false-positive rates?
options: A) Local Outlier Factor (LOF) calculated using Gower distance over the entire transaction history.
B) One-Class Support Vector Machine (OC-SVM) with a Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel.
C) Isolation Forest (iForest) using randomized subspace partitioning.
D) Mahalanobis Distance calculation using a dynamic, online covariance matrix update.
questions.correct_answer_text: C) Isolation Forest (iForest) using randomized subspace partitioning.
questions.explanation: Isolation Forest (iForest) isolates anomalies instead of profiling normal points. Because it relies on random partitioning of feature subspaces, it scales exceptionally well to high-dimensional spaces (O(t * d) where t is the number of trees and d is the tree depth) and does not suffer from distance-metric degradation. It easily fits the sub-30ms real-time latency budget. In contrast, LOF has a quadratic inference complexity O(N^2) which is too slow for real-time performance. OC-SVM with an RBF kernel has high training and inference computational overhead, and Mahalanobis Distance assumes a unimodal multivariate normal distribution and requires expensive matrix inversions (O(D^3)), which fail to capture complex, non-linear multivariate fraudulent interactions under strict latency.

questions.scenario: Real-world Industrial Problem Statement: A healthcare monitoring system tracks real-time physiological telemetry (heart rate, SpO2, systolic and diastolic blood pressures) from ICU wearable sensors. Due to transient network dropouts and sensor disconnections, the data stream frequently exhibits Missing at Random (MAR) patterns. The downstream clinical alert system uses an ensemble classifier that cannot process missing values. Crucially, the features are highly correlated (e.g., changes in heart rate correlate with blood pressure fluctuations), and the system must maintain an end-to-end imputation latency of under 50 milliseconds to prevent clinical alert delays.
questions.question: Which imputation strategy balances high accuracy (preserving multivariate relationships) with the required real-time computational performance?
options: A) Multivariate Imputation by Chained Equations (MICE) utilizing Predictive Mean Matching with 10 imputations.
B) A single-pass Iterative Imputer using a lightweight Bayesian Ridge Regressor with low iteration limits (e.g., max_iter=2) on a sliding temporal window.
C) MissForest (Random Forest-based iterative imputation) applied dynamically to each incoming micro-batch.
D) K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) Imputation (k=5) using Euclidean distance computed against the entire historical patient database.
questions.correct_answer_text: B) A single-pass Iterative Imputer using a lightweight Bayesian Ridge Regressor with low iteration limits (e.g., max_iter=2) on a sliding temporal window.
questions.explanation: In real-time environments with a sub-50ms latency budget, highly iterative and ensemble-based imputation methods like MissForest and standard MICE (with multiple imputations and predictive mean matching) are computationally prohibitive due to their O(Iterations * Trees/Regressions) complexity. KNN Imputation scales with the size of the reference database O(N * D), making it slow for massive streaming histories. A single-pass Iterative Imputer using a lightweight linear model like Bayesian Ridge Regression over a sliding temporal window models the strong correlations among the vital signs efficiently, ensuring high accuracy while keeping inference time well within the millisecond scale.
